1: παρακαλῶ (I urge/beseech — present indicative) is the characteristic Pauline transition from indicative to imperative: because of the doctrinal foundation (Ephesians 1-3), here is how to live. The tone is exhortation, not command — the same verb used for comfort and consolation. ἀξίως τῆς κλήσεως (worthily of the calling) — the calling established in Ephesians 1:4-14; the conduct must correspond to the identity. δέσμιος ἐν κυρίῳ (prisoner in the Lord — same phrase as Ephesians 3:1) — Paul's imprisonment lends moral authority to the exhortation.
2: ταπεινοφροσύνη (lowliness of mind/humility) — in Greco-Roman ethics this term was often pejorative (servility); Paul revalues it as a virtue essential to community. πραΰτης (meekness/gentleness — strength under control) and μακροθυμία (longsuffering — the long-tempered disposition that bears provocation without retaliation). ἀνεχόμενοι ἀλλήλων (bearing with one another — present participle, continuous) — the ongoing practice of tolerating friction within the body.
3: σπουδάζοντες (making every effort/haste — present participle; urgency) — unity is not automatic; it requires diligent effort. τηρεῖν τὴν ἑνότητα τοῦ πνεύματος (to keep the unity of the Spirit) — the unity is already given by the Spirit (one body, one Spirit); the task is preservation, not creation. ἐν τῷ συνδέσμῳ τῆς εἰρήνης (in the bond of peace) — peace is the ligament that holds the unity together.
4: The seven-fold unity formula begins: ἓν σῶμα καὶ ἓν πνεῦμα (one body, one Spirit) — the body is the church, the Spirit is the animating principle. μία ἐλπίς (one hope) — the eschatological common destiny shared by all members. The abruption in Greek — no verb "there is" — creates a staccato confessional rhythm, almost creedal. Each "one" carries its own weight.
5: εἷς κύριος, μία πίστις, ἓν βάπτισμα — the second triad. κύριος (Lord) — the confessional title of Jesus (Philippians 2:11; Romans 10:9). πίστις (faith) here likely refers to the content of belief (the one faith, the body of trust), not merely the act of trusting. βάπτισμα (baptism) — the one initiatory rite shared by all, Jewish and Gentile alike, in contrast to the divisive rite of circumcision recalled in Ephesians 2:11.
6: εἷς θεός καὶ πατήρ πάντων (one God and Father of all) — the third triad reaches its apex in the Shema-echoing monotheism. Three prepositional phrases qualify the one God: ὁ ἐπὶ πάντων (over all — sovereignty), καὶ διὰ πάντων (through all — agency), καὶ ἐν πᾶσιν (in all — immanence). The prepositional variation prevents any single relation from exhausting the divine presence.
7: ἑκάστῳ δὲ ἡμῶν (but to each one of us — emphatic distribution) pivots from the sevenfold unity to diversity within it. κατὰ τὸ μέτρον τῆς δωρεᾶς τοῦ Χριστοῦ (according to the measure of the gift of Christ) — Christ is both the giver and the standard of the gift. The measure is his sovereign apportionment, not human election or merit.
8: διὸ λέγει (therefore it says — introducing Psalms 68:18 [LXX Ephesians 67:19]) — the citation is introduced without naming the Psalm; the congregation is expected to recognize it. Critically, Paul reads ἔδωκεν δόματα τοῖς ἀνθρώποις (gave gifts to men) where the LXX has "received gifts among men." This deliberate adaptation — gave rather than received — applies the Psalm to the exalted Christ distributing gifts to the church. The ascent/giving logic depends on this reading.
9: τὸ δὲ ἀνέβη (the "he ascended") — Paul interprets the Psalm's "ascent" by inferring a prior descent. The parenthetical argument is: ascent presupposes prior descent; Christ's exaltation presupposes his incarnation/descent. εἰς τὰ κατώτερα μέρη τῆς γῆς (into the lower parts of the earth) — interpreted as the incarnation (earth is lower than heaven) or as death/Hades; the former fits better here since the contrast is ascension above all heavens.
10: ὁ καταβάς αὐτός ἐστιν καὶ ὁ ἀναβάς (the one who descended is himself also the one who ascended) — the same person traverses the full cosmic range: incarnation to exaltation. ὑπεράνω πάντων τῶν οὐρανῶν (far above all the heavens) — in Jewish cosmology there were multiple heavens (2 Corinthians 12:2 mentions a third); Christ's ascent surpasses them all. The purpose: ἵνα πληρώσῃ τὰ πάντα (in order that he might fill all things) — same plēroō root as Ephesians 1:23; the filling is cosmic in scope.
11: αὐτὸς ἔδωκεν (he himself gave — emphatic; Christ is the direct giver of the ministries) — five gifts to the church: ἀποστόλους (apostles), προφήτας (prophets), εὐαγγελιστάς (evangelists), ποιμένας καὶ διδασκάλους (pastors and teachers). The last pair shares a single definite article, possibly indicating a combined role — the pastor who teaches — or at minimum the closest functional overlap of all five.
12: Three purpose clauses with πρός / εἰς / εἰς: πρὸς τὸν καταρτισμόν (for the equipping — katartismos, the outfitting of soldiers or the setting of a bone; restoring to proper function), εἰς ἔργον διακονίας (for works of service/ministry), εἰς οἰκοδομὴν τοῦ σώματος (for the building up of the body of Christ). The direction is outward: the gifted ministries equip the whole people for ministry, which builds the body.
13: εἰς τὴν ἑνότητα τῆς πίστεως (to the unity of the faith) — the telos of the ministry gifts. ἄνδρα τέλειον (mature/perfect man) — anēr here is used corporately (not gender-specifically) for the full-grown humanity constituted by the community; the mature community is the "one new humanity" of 2:15 now fully realized. μέτρον ἡλικίας τοῦ πληρώματος τοῦ Χριστοῦ (measure of the fullness/stature of Christ) — plēroma again; Christ's fullness is the standard of maturity.
14: νήπιοι (infants — here pejoratively: immature, dependent, easily destabilized). κλυδωνιζόμενοι (tossed by waves) and περιφερόμενοι (carried about) — both present passive participles depicting continuous instability. κυβεία (sleight of dice — kubeia, from kubos, dice) applies the image of cheating at dice to doctrinal manipulation; the deceitful methods (μεθοδεία) of error are deliberate and structured, not random.
15: ἀληθεύοντες ἐν ἀγάπῃ (speaking/living the truth in love — present participle; ongoing practice) — alētheuō is broader than speech: holding and enacting truth. The contrast with the deception of Ephesians 4:14 is sharp. αὐξήσωμεν εἰς αὐτόν τὰ πάντα (we may grow into him in all things — he is the direction of growth, not merely its cause): Christ as the head toward whom the body grows, not merely from whom it grows.
16: ἐξ οὗ πᾶν τὸ σῶμα (from whom the whole body) — Christ the head is the origin and organizing principle. συναρμολογούμενον καὶ συμβιβαζόμενον (joined together and held together — present passive participles) — two architectural/anatomical compound verbs; the body has structure (fitted) and integrity (compacted). διὰ πάσης ἁφῆς τῆς ἐπιχορηγίας (through every supporting ligament) — the connective tissue metaphor; each part has a defined supply function that supports the whole.
17: μαρτύρομαι ἐν κυρίῳ (I testify/solemnly urge in the Lord) — the verb is stronger than parakaleō; it carries the weight of an oath-testimony. The contrast with Gentile life (μηκέτι ὑμᾶς περιπατεῖν καθὼς καὶ τὰ ἔθνη) is stark: the Ephesian believers must have a fundamentally different manner of life from the surrounding culture. ματαιότητι τοῦ νοός (futility/vanity of the mind) — the Gentile mind as vain, purposeless, oriented toward nothing.
18: ἐσκοτωμένοι τῇ διανοίᾳ (darkened in their understanding — perfect passive participle, settled state). ἀπηλλοτριωμένοι τῆς ζωῆς τοῦ θεοῦ (alienated from the life of God — same verb as Ephesians 2:12; estrangement is the result). The cause: διὰ τὴν ἄγνοιαν τὴν οὖσαν ἐν αὐτοῖς (through the ignorance that is in them) and διὰ τὴν πώρωσιν τῆς καρδίας αὐτῶν (through the hardening of their heart — pōrōsis, a medical term for the calcification of bone or cartilage; a hardened organ that cannot receive).
19: ἀπηλγηκότες (having become callous/past feeling — perfect active participle; a condition arrived at and now settled). The word means to cease feeling pain — moral anesthesia. ἀσελγείᾳ (sensuality/licentiousness) — the abandonment of social restraint; the word connotes not merely private immorality but public shamelessness. πλεονεξία (greed — always wanting more) pairs with sensuality as the twin engines of this descent.
20: ὑμεῖς δὲ οὐχ οὕτως ἐμάθετε τὸν Χριστόν (but you did not learn Christ in this way — emphatic pronoun hymeis de) — the emphatic contrast sets the Ephesian community decisively apart from the Gentile pattern just described. ἐμάθετε τὸν Χριστόν (learned Christ) — an unusual construction; not "learned about Christ" but "learned Christ" — personal encounter with him as the content of the learning.
21: εἴγε αὐτὸν ἠκούσατε καὶ ἐν αὐτῷ ἐδιδάχθητε (if indeed you heard him and were taught in him) — the conditional is not doubt but affirmation; the two aorists (heard, were taught) describe the once-for-all catechetical formation. καθώς ἐστιν ἀλήθεια ἐν τῷ Ἰησοῦ (as truth is in Jesus) — the unusual use of Iēsou (the human name alone) grounds truth in the historical particularity of the incarnate person, not abstract doctrine.
22: ἀποθέσθαι ὑμᾶς κατὰ τὴν προτέραν ἀναστροφήν (to put off, concerning your former way of life — aorist infinitive, decisive act) — the putting off is a single decisive act, like removing a garment. τὸν παλαιὸν ἄνθρωπον (the old man/humanity) is the corporate old self in Adam. φθειρόμενον κατὰ τὰς ἐπιθυμίας τῆς ἀπάτης (being corrupted by the desires of deceit — present passive participle; the old humanity is in the process of ongoing decay).
23: ἀνανεοῦσθαι δὲ τῷ πνεύματι τοῦ νοός ὑμῶν (to be renewed in the spirit of your mind — present passive infinitive, ongoing process) — the middle term between putting off the old (Ephesians 4:22, aorist) and putting on the new (Ephesians 4:24, aorist). Renewal is continuous. πνεύματι τοῦ νοός (spirit of the mind) is a distinctive genitive — the animating principle or disposition of the mind; it may echo the pneuma/nous distinction in contemporary philosophy, here united rather than opposed.
24: ἐνδύσασθαι τὸν καινὸν ἄνθρωπον (to put on the new humanity — aorist, decisive action). καινός (new in quality, not merely recent — contrast neos) — the new humanity is qualitatively different, not just temporally new. κατὰ θεὸν κτισθέντα (created according to God — cf. Ephesians 2:10; the new self is a divine creation, not human self-improvement). ἐν δικαιοσύνῃ καὶ ὁσιότητι τῆς ἀληθείας (in righteousness and holiness of truth) — dikaiosynē (righteous character toward others) and hosiotēs (holiness/piety toward God) as the twin dimensions of the new life.
25: ἀποθέμενοι τὸ ψεῦδος (having put off falsehood — aorist participle; the decisive removal that precedes what follows) and λαλεῖτε ἀλήθειαν (speak truth — present imperative, ongoing). The specific ground: ὅτι ἐσμὲν ἀλλήλων μέλη (because we are members of one another). Honesty is not merely ethical but ecclesiological — lying to a fellow member is a form of self-harm to the body.
26: ὀργίζεσθε καὶ μὴ ἁμαρτάνετε (be angry and do not sin) — a quotation of Psalms 4:4 LXX. The construction is best read as concessive imperative: "if you are angry, do not sin" or "even if anger comes, do not let it lead to sin." Paul is not commanding anger but acknowledging its reality and limiting its damage. ὁ ἥλιος μὴ ἐπιδυέτω (let not the sun go down — prohibitive imperative) sets an urgency: anger resolved daily, not nursed into settled hostility.
27: μηδὲ δίδοτε τόπον τῷ διαβόλῳ (and give no place/foothold to the devil — present imperative prohibition) — topos (place) can be a spatial location, a legal opportunity, or a beachhead. Unresolved anger creates an opening (topos) that becomes an exploitable position. The connection between anger management and spiritual warfare is not incidental; Paul treats them as directly linked.
28: ὁ κλέπτων μηκέτι κλεπτέτω (the one who steals, let him steal no more — present participle and present imperative; the thief is characterized by his ongoing habit, and the command ends it). κοπιάτω ἐργαζόμενος τὸ ἀγαθόν (let him labor, working the good — present participle emphasizing the ongoing character of legitimate work). The purpose of honest labor: ἵνα ἔχῃ μεταδιδόναι (in order that he might have something to share) — work is oriented not toward accumulation but redistribution.
29: λόγος σαπρός (rotten/corrupt word — sapros is used of rotten fruit and fish) — speech that is decayed, worthless. The alternative: εἴ τις ἀγαθὸς πρὸς οἰκοδομήν (whatever is good for building up — oikodomē, same word as the body-building in Ephesians 4:12,16) — speech is either demolition or construction. κατὰ τὴν χρείαν (according to the need) — the criterion is the hearer's specific situation, not the speaker's self-expression.
30: μὴ λυπεῖτε τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον (do not grieve the Holy Spirit — present imperative prohibition; stop or do not start grieving) — the Spirit is a person who can be grieved; the prohibition implies relational damage. The ground clause: ἐν ᾧ ἐσφραγίσθητε (in whom you were sealed — aorist passive, same verb as Ephesians 1:13) — the sealing is the basis for the appeal; to grieve the Spirit is to damage the relationship that guarantees the inheritance.
31: Six terms for the corrosive interior and its expression: πικρία (bitterness — the root; the settled inner disposition of resentment), θυμός (passion/rage — sudden explosion of anger), ὀργή (anger — settled, burning anger), κραυγή (outcry/clamor — the vocal expression), βλασφημία (slander/abusive speech), and κακία (malice — the comprehensive term for wickedness). The list moves from interior attitude through verbal expression to general disposition.
32: χαριζόμενοι ἑαυτοῖς (forgiving one another — present participle; ongoing practice; charizomai from charis, grace) — forgiveness is literally grace-giving. The model: καθὼς καὶ ὁ θεὸς ἐν Χριστῷ ἐχαρίσατο ὑμῖν (just as God in Christ forgave you — aorist; the divine forgiveness is the completed event that establishes the pattern and the power for human forgiveness). The ethical imperative is grounded in the indicative of divine action.
